para.text: Applications commonly use cryptography to implement authentication mechanisms and to ensure the confidentiality and integrity of sensitive data, both in transit and at rest. The proper and accurate implementation of cryptography is extremely critical to its efficacy. Configuration or coding mistakes as well as incorrect assumptions may negate a large degree of the protection it affords, leaving the crypto implementation vulnerable to attack.

Common cryptographic mistakes include, but are not limited to, selecting weak keys or weak cipher modes, unintentionally exposing sensitive cryptographic data, using predictable entropy sources, and mismanaging or hard-coding keys.

Developers often make the dangerous assumption that they can improve security by designing their own cryptographic algorithm; however, one of the basic tenets of cryptography is that any cipher whose effectiveness is reliant on the secrecy of the algorithm is fundamentally flawed.

Select the appropriate type of cryptography for the intended purpose. Avoid proprietary encryption algorithms as they typically rely on "security through obscurity" rather than sound mathematics. Select key sizes appropriate for the data being protected; for high assurance applications, 256-bit symmetric keys and 2048-bit asymmetric keys are sufficient. Follow best practices for key storage, and ensure that plaintext data and key material are not inadvertently exposed.

text.text: The SSL Certificate Details provide information about the certificate associated with the HTTPS server. This information describes important attributes of the certificate and should be reviewed for the correctness of the contact information, whether it is self-signed, and how soon it will expire. The Supported Ciphers provides information about each available encryption scheme available for each of the possible SSL/TLS protocols.

flaw.description:
In this codepath (requests.get), strict verification of the SSL/TLS certificate chain is disabled. Failing to validate the certificate chain leaves the connection vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks.
Always enable strict validation of certificates when using SSL/TLS.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/295.html)

mitigation.description:
These are false positive and there is no improper certificate validation involved here.
Please see last approval as evidence: 11/17/2025 11:49:16 Tarun Aggarwal Approve Mitigation Approved because all connections are used in test cases and local environments, as discussed with the team.This satisfies the requirements for mitigate by design.
Approved because all connections are used in test cases and local environments, as discussed with the team.This satisfies the requirements for mitigate by design.

annotation.description:
These are false positive and there is no improper certificate validation involved here.
Please see last approval as evidence: 11/17/2025 11:49:16 Tarun Aggarwal Approve Mitigation Approved because all connections are used in test cases and local environments, as discussed with the team.This satisfies the requirements for mitigate by design.

flaw.description:
In this codepath (requests.get), strict verification of the SSL/TLS certificate chain is disabled. Failing to validate the certificate chain leaves the connection vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks.
Always enable strict validation of certificates when using SSL/TLS.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/295.html)

Approved because all connections are used in test cases and local environments, as discussed with the team.This satisfies the requirements for mitigate by design.

para.text: Applications commonly use cryptography to implement authentication mechanisms and to ensure the confidentiality and integrity of sensitive data, both in transit and at rest. The proper and accurate implementation of cryptography is extremely critical to its efficacy. Configuration or coding mistakes as well as incorrect assumptions may negate a large degree of the protection it affords, leaving the crypto implementation vulnerable to attack.

Common cryptographic mistakes include, but are not limited to, selecting weak keys or weak cipher modes, unintentionally exposing sensitive cryptographic data, using predictable entropy sources, and mismanaging or hard-coding keys.

Developers often make the dangerous assumption that they can improve security by designing their own cryptographic algorithm; however, one of the basic tenets of cryptography is that any cipher whose effectiveness is reliant on the secrecy of the algorithm is fundamentally flawed.

Select the appropriate type of cryptography for the intended purpose. Avoid proprietary encryption algorithms as they typically rely on "security through obscurity" rather than sound mathematics. Select key sizes appropriate for the data being protected; for high assurance applications, 256-bit symmetric keys and 2048-bit asymmetric keys are sufficient. Follow best practices for key storage, and ensure that plaintext data and key material are not inadvertently exposed.

text.text: Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.
Proposal approved because the random number is not being used for any security sensitive purpose. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

annotation.description: The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.
Proposal approved because the random number is not being used for any security sensitive purpose. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

annotation.description: The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.
Proposal approved because the random number is not being used for any security sensitive purpose. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

annotation.description: The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: The line wait_time = backoff + random.random() is used to introduce slight jitter to the retry or wait interval to avoid simultaneous client retries (e.g., thundering herd). This is a non-security context, and the randomness does not impact authentication, encryption, or any sensitive data processing. As such, the use of random.random() is intentionally chosen and industry-standard for this kind of operational timing behavior. This flaw is a false positive.
Proposal approved because the random number is not used for any security purposes rather for the retry time which is a non-security context. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design

annotation.description: The line wait_time = backoff + random.random() is used to introduce slight jitter to the retry or wait interval to avoid simultaneous client retries (e.g., thundering herd). This is a non-security context, and the randomness does not impact authentication, encryption, or any sensitive data processing. As such, the use of random.random() is intentionally chosen and industry-standard for this kind of operational timing behavior. This flaw is a false positive.

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: Remediation: If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.

In this decorator, the use of `random.uniform(0, delay * 0.3)` is not for any security-sensitive operation—it’s simply intended to introduce slight variability ("jitter") into the retry timing. The goal is not to generate secrets or tokens, but rather to stagger retry attempts and avoid them clustering at the same moment.

this decorator, the use of random.uniform(0, delay * 0.3) is not for any security-sensitive operation—it’s simply intended to introduce slight variability ("jitter") into the retry timing. The goal is not to generate secrets or tokens, but rather to stagger retry attempts and avoid them clustering at the same moment.
Proposal approved because the random number is not being used for any security sensitive purpose. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

annotation.description: Remediation: If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.

In this decorator, the use of `random.uniform(0, delay * 0.3)` is not for any security-sensitive operation—it’s simply intended to introduce slight variability ("jitter") into the retry timing. The goal is not to generate secrets or tokens, but rather to stagger retry attempts and avoid them clustering at the same moment.

this decorator, the use of random.uniform(0, delay * 0.3) is not for any security-sensitive operation—it’s simply intended to introduce slight variability ("jitter") into the retry timing. The goal is not to generate secrets or tokens, but rather to stagger retry attempts and avoid them clustering at the same moment.

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: These are unit tests where ssl is being turned off. they are not in any of our code, just being used to prove correct functionality for dev integration testing
Rejected since need additional information. Please confirm what the random function is being used for. If it is being used for security purpose then use cryptographic alternatives like secrets

The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.
Approved since the random function is not being used for authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions.It is being used for load distribution.

annotation.description: These are unit tests where ssl is being turned off. they are not in any of our code, just being used to prove correct functionality for dev integration testing

The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.
Proposal approved because the random number is not being used for any security sensitive purpose. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

annotation.description: The use of `random.uniform` in this code is a false positive finding. It is applied only to introduce timing jitter (e.g., for backoff or load distribution) and is not involved in authentication, authorization, or cryptographic functions. Predictability of these values does not pose a security risk.

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: The use of random.uniform in this line is part of an exponential backoff mechanism with jitter. It introduces variability in retry timing to avoid synchronized retries from overwhelming systems. This use is not related to any security-sensitive operation (e.g., no secret/token generation or cryptographic use). The random module is used here in a non-security context, and this is an industry-accepted pattern. Therefore, the flagged flaw is a false positive.
Proposal approved because the random number is not being used for any security sensitive purpose. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

annotation.description: The use of random.uniform in this line is part of an exponential backoff mechanism with jitter. It introduces variability in retry timing to avoid synchronized retries from overwhelming systems. This use is not related to any security-sensitive operation (e.g., no secret/token generation or cryptographic use). The random module is used here in a non-security context, and this is an industry-accepted pattern. Therefore, the flagged flaw is a false positive.

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: The use of random.randint in this line is for generating a temporary file name during an upload process. It is not used in any security-sensitive context such as authentication, authorization, or secret generation. The goal is to reduce the chance of filename collisions within a temporary directory. This is an operational pattern, not a cryptographic one. Thus, the entropy concern is a false positive for this usage.
Proposal approved because the random number is not being used for any security sensitive purpose. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

annotation.description: The use of random.randint in this line is for generating a temporary file name during an upload process. It is not used in any security-sensitive context such as authentication, authorization, or secret generation. The goal is to reduce the chance of filename collisions within a temporary directory. This is an operational pattern, not a cryptographic one. Thus, the entropy concern is a false positive for this usage.

flaw.description:
Standard random number generators do not provide a sufficient amount of entropy when used for security purposes. Attackers can brute force the output of pseudorandom number generators such as rand().
If this random number is used where security is a concern, such as generating passwords, session keys, authentication etc, use a trusted cryptographic random number generator instead. The new secrets module introduced in Python 3.6, is used for generating such cryptographically strong random number generators.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/331.html) secrets module (https://docs.python.org/3/library/secrets.html#module-secrets) cryptography.io (https://cryptography.io/en/latest/)

mitigation.description: The use of random.uniform() here is for adding jitter to retry backoff delays during transient failure handling (e.g., HTTP or S3 upload retries). It is not used for authentication, session management, token generation, or any security-critical logic. This usage follows standard retry patterns and is not a source of exploitable entropy. Therefore, the flaw is a false positive and presents no security risk in context.

The flagged line uses AES in CBC mode to decrypt a payload that was encrypted using hybrid RSA + AES encryption. This AES-CBC is not used on its own, but as a short-lived, one-time encryption layer, where:
The AES key is asymmetrically protected via RSA encryption (using PKCS1v1.5).
A new IV is used with each encryption and is correctly passed alongside the payload.
The decrypted data is strictly validated using json.loads() and rejected on any tampering or padding anomaly.
The decrypted content is not persisted or reused.
While AES-GCM (authenticated encryption) is generally preferred, this implementation is safe in context and follows standard hybrid encryption practices. Therefore, this is a false positive, and the cryptographic use is well-controlled and secure for its purpose.
Approved since because the random function is not used for any security purposes rather for the retry time.

annotation.description: The use of random.uniform() here is for adding jitter to retry backoff delays during transient failure handling (e.g., HTTP or S3 upload retries). It is not used for authentication, session management, token generation, or any security-critical logic. This usage follows standard retry patterns and is not a source of exploitable entropy. Therefore, the flaw is a false positive and presents no security risk in context.

The flagged line uses AES in CBC mode to decrypt a payload that was encrypted using hybrid RSA + AES encryption. This AES-CBC is not used on its own, but as a short-lived, one-time encryption layer, where:
The AES key is asymmetrically protected via RSA encryption (using PKCS1v1.5).
A new IV is used with each encryption and is correctly passed alongside the payload.
The decrypted data is strictly validated using json.loads() and rejected on any tampering or padding anomaly.
The decrypted content is not persisted or reused.
While AES-GCM (authenticated encryption) is generally preferred, this implementation is safe in context and follows standard hybrid encryption practices. Therefore, this is a false positive, and the cryptographic use is well-controlled and secure for its purpose.

cwe.cwename: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm

text.text: The use of a broken or risky cryptographic algorithm is an unnecessary risk that may result in the disclosure of sensitive information.

flaw.categoryname: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
flaw.description:
This function uses the cryptography.hazmat.primitives.ciphers.algorithms.AES() function, which uses a hash algorithm that is considered weak. In recent years, researchers have demonstrated ways to breach many uses of previously-thought-safe hash functions such as MD5.
Consider using a stronger algorithm in order to prevent attackers from being able to manipulate hash results. If this algorithm is being used to hash passwords, then consider using a strong computationally-hard algorithm such as PBKDF2 or bcrypt instead of a plain hashing algorithm.
References: CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/327.html)

mitigation.description:
The flagged line uses AES in CBC mode to decrypt a payload that was encrypted using hybrid RSA + AES encryption. This AES-CBC is not used on its own, but as a short-lived, one-time encryption layer, where:
The AES key is asymmetrically protected via RSA encryption (using PKCS1v1.5).
A new IV is used with each encryption and is correctly passed alongside the payload.
The decrypted data is strictly validated using json.loads() and rejected on any tampering or padding anomaly.
The decrypted content is not persisted or reused.
While AES-GCM (authenticated encryption) is generally preferred, this implementation is safe in context and follows standard hybrid encryption practices. Therefore, this is a false positive, and the cryptographic use is well-controlled and secure for its purpose.
Approved since the use of AES in CBC mode within a hybrid RSA + AES system encrypts the payloads.

annotation.description:
The flagged line uses AES in CBC mode to decrypt a payload that was encrypted using hybrid RSA + AES encryption. This AES-CBC is not used on its own, but as a short-lived, one-time encryption layer, where:
The AES key is asymmetrically protected via RSA encryption (using PKCS1v1.5).
A new IV is used with each encryption and is correctly passed alongside the payload.
The decrypted data is strictly validated using json.loads() and rejected on any tampering or padding anomaly.
The decrypted content is not persisted or reused.
While AES-GCM (authenticated encryption) is generally preferred, this implementation is safe in context and follows standard hybrid encryption practices. Therefore, this is a false positive, and the cryptographic use is well-controlled and secure for its purpose.

para.text: The application's supporting infrastructure, including web servers and application servers, can impact the security of the deployed application. Failing to lock down a server, for example, can result in information leaks via error pages, stack traces, or unnecessary files left in a web-accessible directory. Even though these servers are not part of the application codebase, they create insecurities in the environment which contribute to overall risk.

Remove all extraneous files, including demonstration applications and sample code, from production systems. Configure production servers with the minimum set of services required for the application to function, and ensure that information leaks do not occur via server-generated error pages.
Audit any third party dependencies or services that are deployed by default to ensure that they do not compromise the security of the application being supported.

cwe.cwename: Selection of Less-Secure Algorithm During Negotiation ('Algorithm Downgrade')

text.text: A protocol or its implementation supports interaction between multiple actors and allows those actors to negotiate which algorithm should be used as a protection mechanism such as encryption or authentication, but it does not select the strongest algorithm that is available to both parties.

flaw.categoryname: Selection of Less-Secure Algorithm During Negotiation ('Algorithm Downgrade')
flaw.description:
Explicitly weaker protocol is used as channel encryption protocol. This downgrade to a weaker protocol, could make it easier for attackers to compromise the system.
Based on the implementation use, make sure the channel communication is over at least TLS protocol. Ideally use TLS 1.3 if available, otherwise use TLS 1.2.
CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/757.html)

mitigation.description: Technique : GP1 : Use libraries and frameworks that make it easier to avoid introducing weaknesses Specifics : We will fix of context.minimum_version = ssl.TLSVersion.TLSv1_2, we'll have to test if SMTP endpoint in each environment supports TLS 1.2 or newer and do another round of regression in the next release Remaining Risk : none Verification : There are no passwords or secrets exposed in the current solution
Proposal rejected because the proposed mitigation does not address the flaw. The application team is planning to mitigate the finding in future, but the issue is still present in the code.

Item has been addressed :
context = ssl.create_default_context() # Enforce TLS 1.2 as minimum to prevent protocol downgrade attacks (CWE-757) context.minimum_version = ssl.TLSVersion.TLSv1_2 # Restrict cipher suites to strong algorithms only - prevent cipher downgrade attacks (CWE-757) # Excludes: NULL, export, DES, RC4, MD5-based, anonymous ciphers context.set_ciphers( "ECDHE+AESGCM:DHE+AESGCM:ECDHE+CHACHA20:DHE+CHACHA20:!aNULL:!eNULL:!EXPORT:!DES:!3DES:!RC4:!MD5:!PSK:!SRP:!CAMELLIA" )
Proposal approved because TLS v1.2 has been implemented. Meets the requirements for Mitigation by Design.

annotation.description: We will fix of context.minimum_version = ssl.TLSVersion.TLSv1_2, we'll have to test if SMTP endpoint in each environment supports TLS 1.2 or newer and do another round of regression in the next release
IPT0026093.pdf from RITM24805719 are for this item

Item has been addressed :
context = ssl.create_default_context() # Enforce TLS 1.2 as minimum to prevent protocol downgrade attacks (CWE-757) context.minimum_version = ssl.TLSVersion.TLSv1_2 # Restrict cipher suites to strong algorithms only - prevent cipher downgrade attacks (CWE-757) # Excludes: NULL, export, DES, RC4, MD5-based, anonymous ciphers context.set_ciphers( "ECDHE+AESGCM:DHE+AESGCM:ECDHE+CHACHA20:DHE+CHACHA20:!aNULL:!eNULL:!EXPORT:!DES:!3DES:!RC4:!MD5:!PSK:!SRP:!CAMELLIA" )

flaw.categoryname: Selection of Less-Secure Algorithm During Negotiation ('Algorithm Downgrade')
flaw.description:
Explicitly weaker protocol is used as channel encryption protocol. This downgrade to a weaker protocol, could make it easier for attackers to compromise the system.
Based on the implementation use, make sure the channel communication is over at least TLS protocol. Ideally use TLS 1.3 if available, otherwise use TLS 1.2.
CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/757.html)

mitigation.description: The SSL context enforces TLS ≥ 1.2 with legacy SSL/TLS versions explicitly disabled. Certificate and hostname verification are mandatory, ensuring server authenticity. Compression is disabled, and only AEAD ciphers (GCM/CHACHA20) are permitted. An optional corporate CA is loaded only for internal services, avoiding global overrides. The scanner flagged this due to pattern-matching on SSLContext creation without analyzing subsequent hardening. In runtime, no downgrade or weak cipher negotiation is possible.
Approved because SSL context enforces TLS ≥ 1.2 with legacy SSL/TLS versions explicitly disabled Meet the requirements for mitigation by design.

annotation.description: The SSL context enforces TLS ≥ 1.2 with legacy SSL/TLS versions explicitly disabled. Certificate and hostname verification are mandatory, ensuring server authenticity. Compression is disabled, and only AEAD ciphers (GCM/CHACHA20) are permitted. An optional corporate CA is loaded only for internal services, avoiding global overrides. The scanner flagged this due to pattern-matching on SSLContext creation without analyzing subsequent hardening. In runtime, no downgrade or weak cipher negotiation is possible.

flaw.categoryname: Selection of Less-Secure Algorithm During Negotiation ('Algorithm Downgrade')
flaw.description:
Explicitly weaker protocol is used as channel encryption protocol. This downgrade to a weaker protocol, could make it easier for attackers to compromise the system.
Based on the implementation use, make sure the channel communication is over at least TLS protocol. Ideally use TLS 1.3 if available, otherwise use TLS 1.2.
CWE (https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/757.html)

mitigation.description: The SSL context enforces TLS ≥ 1.2 with legacy SSL/TLS versions explicitly disabled. Certificate and hostname verification are mandatory, ensuring server authenticity. Compression is disabled, and only AEAD ciphers (GCM/CHACHA20) are permitted. An optional corporate CA is loaded only for internal services, avoiding global overrides. The scanner flagged this due to pattern-matching on SSLContext creation without analyzing subsequent hardening. In runtime, no downgrade or weak cipher negotiation is possible.
Approved because SSL context enforces TLS ≥ 1.2 with legacy SSL/TLS versions explicitly disabled Meet the requirements for mitigation by design.

annotation.description: The SSL context enforces TLS ≥ 1.2 with legacy SSL/TLS versions explicitly disabled. Certificate and hostname verification are mandatory, ensuring server authenticity. Compression is disabled, and only AEAD ciphers (GCM/CHACHA20) are permitted. An optional corporate CA is loaded only for internal services, avoiding global overrides. The scanner flagged this due to pattern-matching on SSLContext creation without analyzing subsequent hardening. In runtime, no downgrade or weak cipher negotiation is possible.

mitigation.description: Team confirmed not modified and dynamically linked

LGPL; team confirmed code is not modified and is dynamically linked

Team confirmed not modified and dynamically linked
Team confirmed not modified and dynamically linked

license.name: GNU Library General Public License v2 or later

mitigation.description: Team confirmed not modified and dynamically linked
Team confirmed not modified and dynamically linked

vulnerability.cve_summary: A security flaw has been discovered in vibrantlabsai RAGAS up to 0.4.3. The affected element is the function _try_process_local_file/_try_process_url of the file src/ragas/metrics/collections/multi_modal_faithfulness/util.py of the component Collections Module. Performing a manipulation of the argument retrieved_contexts results in server-side request forgery. The attack can be initiated remotely. The exploit has been released to the public and may be used for attacks. The security patch for CVE-2025-45691 was applied to a different module only. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any way.

license.name: GNU Lesser General Public License v3.0 only

mitigation.description: Team confirmed not modified and dynamically linked
Team confirmed not modified and dynamically linked
